title: Parsing strategies and their trade-offs
description: Recursive-descent/PEG parsers are hand-written and flexible; LR/LALR are generated and handle more grammars but give opaque conflicts.
body: Use when choosing or debugging a parser. Recursive-descent (with Pratt for expression precedence) is readable, gives great error messages, and is easy to extend but requires the grammar to be LL-friendly; LR/LALR generators accept a broader class of grammars but report shift/reduce conflicts that are hard to diagnose; PEG removes ambiguity by ordered choice but can hide it. Do not pick a strategy without considering error-message quality and grammar shape. Actions: match the strategy to the grammar and the diagnostics you need. Validation signals include the parser accepting the language and producing clear errors on malformed input. Risk boundary: a grammar ambiguity silently resolved the wrong way changes the language; resolve conflicts explicitly rather than ignoring generator warnings.
